subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 6. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your role is to talk, show, point, and play alongside the child using the simple scripts and activities in this guide. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Use position words correctly: on, under, above, below, behind, in front of, next to, between.
Begin to use beside, and start to tell left from right.
Recognise and use ordinal numbers from 1st to 10th.
Say which object is first, last, or a given position in a line.
Follow a simple instruction that uses a position word (‘Put the cup under the mat’).
Give their own position instruction for someone else to follow.
Describe where an object is in relation to another object.
Put everyday events in order using 1st, 2nd, 3rd (a daily routine).
A few small toys or objects the child can hold and move: a toy animal, a ball, a cup, a spoon, a stone.

concepts.title: On, Under, and the First Position Words
concepts.explanation: Start with the two clearest, most physical words: on and under. These are easy to feel because they involve touch — something rests on a surface, or is tucked under it. Use one object and one surface, and move the object as you name where it is. How to teach it: Put a cup on the mat and say clearly, ‘The cup is on the mat.’ Then move it under the low table: ‘Now the cup is under the table.’ Say the position word a little louder than the rest of the sentence so the child hears which word is doing the work. Do this…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Watch. I put the ball ON the chair.” (place it) “Where is the ball?” Child: “On the chair!” You: “Yes! Now I put the ball UNDER the chair.” (place it) You: “Your turn. Put the spoon UNDER the…

concepts.title: Above, Below, and Between
concepts.explanation: Above and below are like on and under, but the objects do not touch — the bird is above the tree, high in the sky; the roots are below the ground. Between means right in the middle of two things, so it always needs two objects on either side. How to teach it: Hold a toy bird high over a toy tree and say ‘The bird is above the tree.’ Lower your other hand under the table: ‘My hand is below the table.’ For between, line up two cups and place a single date in the middle: ‘The date is between the two cups.’ Let the…
concepts.example: Children sometimes muddle above/below with on/under. Gently point out the difference: on touches, above does not. You do not need the child to explain this in words — just to place and point correctly when you ask.

concepts.title: Position Depends on Where You Stand
concepts.explanation: This is the most important — and trickiest — idea in the unit. Position words like in front of, behind, left and right can change depending on your point of view. What is in front of you may be behind me if we face each other. Young children naturally think their own view is the only view, so this takes time and gentle demonstration. How to teach it: Stand a toy on the table facing the child. Ask, ‘Is the toy in front of you or behind you?’ Then walk around to the other side and ask again from your new spot. Show…
concepts.example: Left and right are introduced here very gently. Most five-year-olds are only beginning to tell left from right, and many adults still pause to think. Pick the child's writing or eating hand and name it often: ‘This is…

concepts.title: Ordinal Numbers — 1st to 10th
concepts.explanation: Ordinal numbers tell us which one in an order: first, second, third, and so on. This is different from counting numbers, which tell us how many. Three dates is a quantity; the 3rd date is a position. Children meet ordinal numbers best in real orderings — a line of children, a row of cars, a race, or the steps of the day. How to teach it: Line up five toys or five dates. Point to each in turn from the front and say ‘first, second, third, fourth, fifth.’ Then ask, ‘Which one is third? Point to it.’ Start with just…
concepts.example: A powerful everyday link is the daily routine. Talk through the order of the morning: ‘1st we wake up, 2nd we get dressed, 3rd we wash, 4th we eat.’ Ordering familiar events helps the child feel that ordinal numbers…

concepts.title: Following and Giving Position Instructions
concepts.explanation: The real goal of this unit is that the child can both follow a position instruction and give one. Understanding grows fastest when the child gets to be the one in charge. Simple games make this natural and fun. How to teach it: Play a treasure game. Hide a small toy and give one clue at a time: ‘Look under the mat… now look behind the basket.’ Then swap roles — let the child hide the toy and tell you where to look. When the child gives you an instruction, follow it exactly, even a silly one, so they see that their…
concepts.example: A gentle obstacle course is another favourite: crawl under a chair, walk between two cushions, stand behind the box, finish in front of the door. Movement makes the words stick far better than pointing at pictures.

activities.title: Practise together
activities.materials: Paper, pencil and familiar household objects
steps: Choose one idea from this unit.
Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

questions.question: What does “on / under” mean?
options: On top of, or underneath, something
Higher than, or lower than (not touching)
At the back of, or at the front of
questions.answer: On top of, or underneath, something
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: on / under means On top of, or underneath, something.

questions.question: What does “above / below” mean?
options: Higher than, or lower than (not touching)
At the back of, or at the front of
In the middle of two things
questions.answer: Higher than, or lower than (not touching)
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: above / below means Higher than, or lower than (not touching).

questions.question: What does “behind / in front of” mean?
options: At the back of, or at the front of
In the middle of two things
The very front, or the very end, of an order
questions.answer: At the back of, or at the front of
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: behind / in front of means At the back of, or at the front of.

questions.question: What does “next to / beside” mean?
options: In the middle of two things
The very front, or the very end, of an order
A number that tells position, not how many
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: next to / beside means Right alongside, on either side.

questions.question: What does “between” mean?
options: In the middle of two things
The very front, or the very end, of an order
A number that tells position, not how many
questions.answer: In the middle of two things
questions.hint: Use the Unit 6 Math Words & Symbols reference.
questions.explanation: between means In the middle of two things.

selfAssessment: I can use position words correctly: on, under, above, below, behind, in front of, next to, between.
I can begin to use beside, and start to tell left from right.
I can recognise and use ordinal numbers from 1st to 10th.
I can say which object is first, last, or a given position in a line.
I can follow a simple instruction that uses a position word (‘Put the cup under the mat’).
I can give their own position instruction for someone else to follow.
I can describe where an object is in relation to another object.
I can put everyday events in order using 1st, 2nd, 3rd (a daily routine).
